title: Milking a dairy goat
subtitle: The hand-milking routine for a smallholder's dairy doe.
excerpt: Hand-milking a goat takes five to ten minutes per animal once the routine is established. Getting the technique right from the start matters: incorrect stripping causes teat soreness that leads to avoidance behaviour. This covers the milking stand, the approach sequence, the stripping method, and the milk-handling steps after.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code for Goats (2012). Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (10th edition), goat milking chapter. SAC Consulting Scotland, small-scale dairy goat guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: An adult female goat. After kidding, a doe produces milk for five to eight months in a typical non-intensively managed dairy year.
The small opening at the tip of the teat through which milk flows. Kept closed by a muscle ring between milkings; pressing on the teat base traps milk in the teat before it is stripped out.
Inflammation of the udder, usually bacterial. In dairy goats, presented as hot, firm, lumpy udder tissue and watery or clotted milk from the affected half.

The hormonal release of oxytocin that causes milk to flow from the alveoli into the teats. Triggered by the milking routine itself: the familiar stand, the smell of feed, and the first touch of the udder.

content.text: giving two litres a day at peak lactation needs milking twice daily at consistent intervals, typically 12 hours apart. Consistency of timing matters: late milkings cause udder discomfort and can reduce subsequent production. The milking stand removes the need to restrain the goat by hand; it elevates the udder to a comfortable working height.
Put a measure of concentrate feed in the head stanchion tray before the goat approaches. The routine triggers

via conditioned reflex: the smell of feed, the stand, and the first udder contact all contribute. Lead the doe onto the stand and close the stanchion around her neck. She should settle into eating while you work.

Wash your hands. Wipe each teat with a clean damp cloth. Strip two or three squirts of fore-milk into a small cup and look at it: clear or white fluid is normal; watery, stringy, or clotted fluid is a sign of
in that half and the udder needs to be stripped out fully and the doe checked by a vet. Discard the fore-milk.

by pressing the thumb and forefinger firmly across the base of the teat. Then squeeze the remaining fingers in a wave from index to little finger, forcing the milk downward and out through the teat opening. Release, and the teat refills from the udder. This is the wrist-squeeze method: no pulling or sliding, which pulls teat skin and causes soreness.

Work both teats alternately. A relaxed, well-fed doe on a familiar stand yields consistently; a nervous doe or one with a sore teat will hold milk back. Allow at least five minutes per milking and finish by pressing both hands gently upward under the udder: if more milk flows, the doe is not yet empty.

Dip each teat in a teat-dip solution (iodine-based, commercially available) immediately after the last strip. The teat canal takes 20 to 30 minutes to close fully after milking; dipping during this window prevents bacteria entering. Pour the collected milk through a straining cloth into a clean stainless-steel pail and refrigerate at once. Goat milk chills and develops off-flavours from the milking environment more quickly than cow milk if left warm.
